executive_summary:
This report contains the final evaluation of the project entitled: ‘Towards a Green Economy in
Uruguay: Stimulating Sustainable Production Practices and Low Emission Technologies in
Priority Sectors’, locally known as ‘Biovalor’. The project was implemented from November 2013
to December 2020, with a total cost of US$30.5 million, of which US$3.4 million were financed by
a grant from the Global Environment Facility (GEF) through the United Nations Industrial
Development Organization (UNIDO).
The evaluation was carried out following the methodology and criteria established by UNIDO for
projects financed by the GEF. This summary provides a brief analysis of project results and ratings,
as well as a discussion on the main recommendations.
Based on the comprehensive assessment conducted, the overall project rating was considered
Satisfactory. Taking into account some limitations observed in project design and other
operational shortcomings, this rating reflects the adequate level of achievement of objectives, and
the fact that project implementation exceeded expectations in several of the goals set for each
component and generated several positive results, with good possibilities to sustain and expand
them in the future.
The table below summarizes the scores awarded for each criterion, as well as the overall project
score.

xi

The Biovalor project is considered highly relevant to Uruguay and fully consistent with the
country’s existing policies and regulatory framework. To a large extent thanks to the effective
institutional articulation achieved, the efficient management of the Project Management Unit
(PMU), and the exemplary support provided by UNIDO's local representation, the project
contributed to reducing the main barriers identified in the theory of change and showed the
necessary flexibility to adapt to the changing conditions experienced during implementation as a
commendable feature.
Despite its relatively complex design, the project was effective and managed to meet the
objectives originally set, reducing GHG emissions and significantly improving waste management,
promoting a shift towards environmentally sustainable development and, in particular, towards
a model of circular economy and lower emissions.
The fulfilment of project objectives was achieved through the synergies and coordinated
implementation of its five components. Specifically, the project strengthened environmental
policies, the regulatory framework and the knowledge base on waste conversion and recovery,
fostered private sector investments in technologies relevant to the project's objective, developed
capacities (both within academia and the public and private sectors), and devoted considerable
efforts to communicate and disseminate the knowledge gained.
Through the implementation of pilot subprojects, four specific technologies (composting,
anaerobic digestion, nutrient circularity, and biofuels) were tested on existing commercial
operations with satisfactory results. Although not considered sophisticated, these technologies
were appropriate and innovative for the development stage of waste valorization in the country.
As such, these subprojects provided concrete examples of success, showing the technical and
economic feasibility of adopting the technologies tested.
In terms of sustainability, the evaluation conducted makes it possible to conclude that
reasonable conditions exist for the various contributions of Biovalor to be supported after project
completion, therefore generating a solid basis for achieving positive long-term impacts. The
potential impact resulting from the project depends to a large extent on the sustainability of the
instruments developed or promoted for the consolidation and possible expansion of Biovalor
actions within public sector priorities and the subsequent adoption of these practices by the
private sector. For this to materialize, active public interventions will be needed to continue the
development of policies, instruments, and knowledge, as well as to promote the massive adoption
of successful experiences by the private sector.
Regarding the project’s potential impact, the significant progress made by the project in terms
of regulatory framework, institutional development and technological demonstration suggest that
the implementation of Biovalor should have a significant impact on Uruguay's environmental
performance, mainly through the adoption of sustainable models of low-carbon production and
waste processing. The potential impact of the technologies applied in the pilot projects supported
was assessed in relation to their economic feasibility, possibilities of replication, waste and/or
emission reductions, and employment opportunities, generally providing positive projections.
Beyond the successful implementation achieved, this conclusion is supported by the fact that the
project was fully inserted into an existing public policy, the National Environmental System,
created in 2016 with the purpose of promoting improved linkages between productive sectors
and environmental protection, water management and climate change, as well as the reduction of
pollution problems affecting the country's main water resources.
In terms of quality at entry, regardless of the satisfactory results achieved, some shortcomings
were identified in the design of the project which, to some extent, affected the effectiveness and
efficiency of implementation. These include the complexity of the proposed development
objective, the absence of a specific target for waste processing, certain lack of realism with respect
to the implementation timeline and a particular interpretation and operationalization of the
concept of project co-financing.
xii

As for project implementation, based on the detailed information provided, the analysis of all
factors related to financial parameters suggests that project funds were efficiently utilized and
administered, well complemented by a commendable level of project governance and
coordination. However, certain areas subject to improvement were identified, including the low
degree of implementation in the early years of the project, the lack of interventions in some high-
generating waste sectors (i.e., feedlots and dairy processing industry), the limited dissemination
of calls for expression of interest for pilot projects and the late implementation of most
subprojects, significantly reducing the possibilities of adequately evaluating the benefits of the
different technologies and their dissemination to other potential stakeholders.
In summary, the Biovalor Project can be rated as Satisfactory, basically due to the fact that overall
design expectations were mostly achieved or exceeded in relation to the targets set for each
component. In addition, despite the described operational shortcomings, the project was
instrumental in supporting several positive technological, institutional, and regulatory
improvements, all with reasonable chances of being sustained and expanded in the future. The
successful efforts aimed at improving inter-institutional coordination, developing incentive
mechanisms, and private sector stakeholder engagement, also provides an encouraging scenario
for the identification and promotion of more advanced, higher value-added technologies.
Main Recommendations
o Relevant Uruguayan government authorities should continue to promote, in a coordinated
manner, instruments to encourage replication and adoption of the successful experiences
supported by Biovalor. While significant progress has been achieved through the
implementation of the pilot projects, it is considered of great relevance that MIEM, MGAP
and DINAMA continue supporting joint efforts to remove barriers that currently limit the
competitiveness and expansion of the technologies tested.
o Similarly, it is necessary to define and develop the potential for generation of even greater
environmental benefits through the promotion of waste processing technologies in other
important production chains in the country.
o The environmental authority, together with the Ministry of Economy and Finance, should
seek further dissemination of existing financial incentives for private investments aimed
at waste processing, low carbon technologies, and circular economy initiatives. Similarly,
interinstitutional efforts are required to develop a technical and tax framework that
promotes the viability and competitiveness of biological products, such as compost.
o Regarding the promotion of circular economy initiated by the project, Biovalor’s
institutions should jointly undertake the necessary efforts to ensure the continuity of the
Circular Opportunities program implemented by ANDE and obtain a budget allocation
similar or larger than the one disbursed in 2019, in order to support the demands of the
private sector, in particular small and medium enterprises.
o Given that the installation of the biodigester was not completed by the closing date, the
pilot subproject of Rincón Blanco must receive active supervision and follow-up by
DINAMA to ensure its proper completion and subsequent operation.
o Similarly, it is necessary to provide adequate follow-up on project-funded nutrient
circularity initiatives implemented by the designated academic and research institutions,
in order to ensure proper generation and dissemination of results.
o In the design of the new project being sponsored by UNIDO in Uruguay it would be
important to incorporate the lessons learnt by the project and allocate resources to
specific actions that would contribute to the consolidation of the results achieved by
Biovalor.
xiii

o In terms of project design and management, UNIDO senior management should assess the
possibility of (i) reviewing the methodology for recruiting and incorporating co-financers
into the costs and results matrix of the project (in compliance with GEF requirements); (ii)
providing additional flexibility for equipment procurement procedures in
pilot/demonstration projects implemented and partly financed by the private sector; (iii)
expanding the scope of the mid-term review of projects in order to assess possible need
for restructuring of project activities, budget allocations and indicators; and (iv) reviewing
internal project management criteria and responsibilities, aimed at establishing the
necessary adjustments to achieve greater decentralization of project monitoring and
management functions.
xiv

I. Objectives, methodology and evaluation process
In compliance with the Terms of Reference of August 24, 2020 (Annex 1), the objective
of this work was to carry out the terminal evaluation of the project entitled: ‘Towards a
Green Economy in Uruguay: Stimulating Sustainable Production Practices and Low
Emission Technologies in Priority Sectors’, locally identified as ‘Biovalor’. The project
was financed by the Global Environment Facility (GEF) and implemented by the United
Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO).
The authors of this document (national consultants) were selected by UNIDO's
Independent Evaluation Division. This independent evaluation was carried out
between September and December 2020 in accordance with the standards of the
institutions mentioned above.
The work covered the entire project period, from its inception on December 1, 2013 to
December 31, 2020, and had three main objectives:
o To evaluate project performance according to the following criteria: design,
relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency, sustainability, impact, inclusion
of cross-cutting aspects (gender, environmental, socio-economic and
management) and institutional performance.
o To identify the main lessons learnt from both successful and unsuccessful
practices.
o To summarize the main project findings and make recommendations to the
government, donors, stakeholders and UNIDO to improve the design and
implementation of future and ongoing projects.
With regard to the scope of this evaluation, it is worth mentioning that it was not an in-
depth audit of the project; it aimed to qualy- and quantitatively analyze whether the
activities carried out contributed to achieving the expected results in the Theory of
Change (ToC) implicit in the project. This concept is described in more detail in the
next chapter.
The evaluation methodology was the one proposed in the Inception Report adopted by
UNIDO on 1st October, 2020. The steps followed and the sources of information
consulted in the process were the following:
 A first round of introductory interviews (6 interviews, 8 people) was conducted
during the first two weeks to better understand the project and identify the most
relevant aspects and stakeholders.
 Attendance to regular Steering Committee meeting to identify visions and
perceptions of the different participating institutions.
 An inception report in the third working week (22/09/2020) describing the
evaluation work plan.
 A second round of interviews (about 13 in total, with 13 people) focused on key
aspects of the project.
1

 Visits to four pilot projects and virtual meeting (due to Covid-19) with leads of a
fifth pilot project and one of the main co-financers. The objective of these visits
was, on the one hand, to confirm the existence and quality of the investments
made and, on the other hand, to talk with the beneficiaries to learn about their
perception of Biovalor and the positive and negative aspects of the support
provided.
 Monitoring of the four final project events in November, where, apart from those
interviewed, there were presentations by 17 other people linked to the project.
 The second annex includes the list of those interviewed, the projects visited and
the speakers at the final events; there is also a list of the guiding questions used
at meetings.
 Together with the interviews, a review of the most important project documents
was carried out. Most of them were provided by UNIDO, while others were
available on the project website; the list of documents can also be found in the
second annex.
 Presentation of preliminary findings at the last Steering Committee meeting
(Dec 17, 2020) and to the Vienna UNIDO evaluation team (January 13, 2021).
 Production of final report.
Regarding methodological limitations, there were no major difficulties, as the main
stakeholders agreed to conduct interviews and answer all questions. It was possible to
visit a representative sample of the pilot projects (despite the pandemic) and all
requested documents were received in time. However, to be cautious in a pandemic
context, most of the interviews were online, and it was not possible to make an in-
person visit to Estancias el Lago. In addition, contact with the manager of Rincón
Blanco pilot project was only possible during the final phase of the evaluation, once
the deadlines for completing the biodigester installation and the monitoring mechanism
to be implemented after the closure of Biovalor were agreed upon.
II. Country and project background
A brief description of the national context
Uruguay is one of the smallest countries in Latin America, with an area of 176,215 km2
and a population of about 3.5 million people. It is characterized by a relatively stable
political and social environment and the highest per capita income in the region.
Regarding economic activity, after the 2002 crisis, the Uruguayan economy grew
steadily for about 15 years. However, in 2019 it remained stagnant and would close
this year with a considerable fall, mainly as a result of Covid-19 pandemic.
Uruguay’s temperate and humid climate, and soils favor the development of
agricultural activities. Considering both, its direct impact and its spillover effect, the
primary sector is a significant component of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Also,
agriculture and livestock exports account for about 70 per cent of total exports.
2

Since the beginning of 2000 there was a strong intensification of agricultural
production, with an increase in the agricultural and forestry areas and in beef and milk
production per hectare, which in turn led to an increase in agro-industrial production.
There was also an increase in the scale and concentration of activities, including an
increase in the number of feedlots.
Environmental challenges
The growth described above highlighted and exacerbated several environmental
issues, such as inadequate waste management and disposal, nutrient and
agrochemicals runoff, water pollution, soil erosion, among others. More specifically,
waste from primary and agro-industrial activities such as sawdust, rice husk and cow
manure were generally laid out on the ground, bagasse burning was a common
practice, and liquid effluents were, in some cases, discharged into water courses
without adequate treatment. In some cases, the treatment of effluents in open lagoons
generated other problems, such as methane emissions produced by natural anaerobic
digestion processes.
It is important to mention that in addition to waste from the primary and industrial
sectors, urban waste management has also been a major challenge. According to the
document endorsed by the GEF, it is estimated that municipal solid waste generation
reached 2,200 tons daily and that 58% was organic matter. Regarding its
management, waste is being disposed of in landfills, outside the Metropolitan Area of
Montevideo (AMM); this results in negative environmental impact due to the fact that
only 3 of the 24 landfills surveyed showed acceptable conditions. In other words, there
was no appropriate use of waste to generate energy or other products.
Finally, livestock is the main source of greenhouse gases (GHGs) in Uruguay, mainly
due to enteric fermentation (according to the Global Warming Potential );
100AR2
however, it should be noted that emissions per unit of product have been decreasing.
On the other hand, the forestry sector is responsible for most of the carbon
sequestration. At international level, national emissions accounted for about 0.04% of
global anthropogenic GHG emissions in 2017, reaching 19,709 GgCO2eq (MVOTMA
and SNRCC, 2019). Even though this figure is very low, the country has committed to
contributing to the reduction of emissions. Also, several of the climate change
mitigation practices and technologies bring about other co-benefits (increased
resilience to negative shocks and environmental benefits) being a solution to some of
the problems mentioned.
Regulatory and institutional framework
The problems mentioned above and an increased awareness about environmental
issues motivated the development of new regulations in Uruguay.
The most prominent policies and regulations that were in force or under development
at the start of the project were described in the document submitted to the GEF. Also,
the team validated that this context existed and that it was relevant and compatible
with Biovalor. The following regulations are to be highlighted:
3

 General Environment Protection Law Nº 17,283, 2000. It promotes
environmental protection through the application of new technologies for waste
treatment, management and valorization.
 Climate Change National Response Plan, 2010. Its main objectives are: to
coordinate national actions to respond to climate change, to introduce sectoral
mitigation and adaptation strategies, to promote mitigation through technology
transfer by promoting investment and access to carbon markets, and to promote
training, capacity building and awareness-raising actions. In addition, the plan
sets out action lines, such as the use of at least 30% of agro-industrial waste to
generate energy.
 National Greenhouse Gas Inventory 2004, 2006, 2008, 2010 and 2012 and the
Third National Communication to the Conference of the Parties to the UNFCCC,
2010 (the fourth and fifth communications were published in 2016 and 2019
respectively); they detail the sources of GHG emissions and removals by sector
and gas.
 National Energy Plan 2008-2030. One of its strategic axes is the diversification
of the energy mix and the increase in the share of renewable energies. Wind
and forest biomass energy production was supported by the GEF through the
United Nations Development Program (UNDP).
 National Plan of Action for Environmentally Sustainable Production and
Consumption 2010-2015, 2010. This plan promotes energy efficiency and
energy diversification and waste management and flows.
 National Waste Management (under development when Biovalor was
formulated) and Atmospheric Emissions Reduction Draft Bills.
 The six Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs) presented by
Uruguay to the UNFCCC in 2012. They encouraged the inclusion of renewable
energy in the mix, the promotion of low-emission technologies and sustainable
housing programs, among others.
 Project Sustainable Management of Natural Resources and Climate Change
(2011), financed by the World Bank and implemented by the MGAP. The
objective of this project is to promote best agricultural and livestock practices
that are environmentally sustainable and climate-smart.
In subsequent years, Uruguay also ratified international agreements (e.g., the Paris
Agreement) and promoted the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) Agenda 2030,
among others.
On the other hand, a new institutional structure was created around the subject through
the National Environmental System, the National Environment Cabinet, the National
Secretariat for Environment, Water and Climate Change (no longer working) and the
National Climate Change Response and Variability System. More recently, on August
27, 2020, some functions within the former Ministry of Housing, Territorial Planning
and the Environment were given to the new Ministry of Environment, prioritizing
environmental issues in public policies.
4

Annex 3 contains an outline summarizing these and other policies developed in recent
years, as well as a diagram of the most relevant institutions related to the subject.
Previous cooperation
At the time of the formulation of Biovalor, Uruguay had already had previous
experiences in the implementation of inter-ministerial initiatives. Examples include the
Production Cabinet, where MGAP and MIEM jointly analyzed the energy sector, and
the National Climate Change Response System, created in 2009 to coordinate and
plan risk prevention, and climate change adaptation and mitigation actions. Among
previous GEF-financed experiences, there were:
PROBIO Project - Electricity Production from Biomass in Uruguay (ID# 3144), involving
the MA (former MVOTMA, through the National Environment Agency - DINAMA), the
MGAP and the MIEM (through the National Energy Agency - DNE). It focused on
decentralized energy generation from biomass, but limiting the treatment of forestry
resources.
Energy Efficiency Project (ID# 1179), whose objective was “to increase the demand
for and competitive supply of energy-efficient goods and services, contributing to: (a)
an increased efficiency in energy use; (b) reduce Uruguayan economy’s dependence
on electricity and imported fuels; and (c) reduce emissions from the energy sector”.
The PEEU - Uruguay Wind Energy Program (ID# 2826), a project that generated
relevant regulatory instruments to stimulate the integration of renewable energy
sources. Small Grants Program, which supported the implementation of effluent
treatment on small dairy farms.
Project management and main changes observed during implementation
The project was managed at institutional and project levels. At the institutional level,
UNIDO was the implementing agency, while the MIEM (through the DNE), the
MVOTMA (through DINAMA) and the MGAP were the executing counterparts.
The governance structure was represented by the Steering Committee (SC) composed
of representatives from the three ministries and UNIDO's representation in Uruguay.
The SC was chaired by the Director of the National Energy Agency and held biannual
meetings. Its responsibilities included: reviewing and approving the annual work plans,
annual reports to the GEF (PIRs) and annual budgets; monitoring project progress,
providing strategic advice, and appointing the project coordinator. In addition, it was in
charge of the creation of a Project Management Unit (PMU), the selection of its
members and its supervision.
According to the project document, these SC institutions were assigned the following
roles:
DNE: Responsible for keeping the project up-to-date and developing specific initiatives
in line with the objectives set. The in-kind contribution by the Ministry of Industry,
Energy and Mining (MIEM), through the DNE, consisted of hosting the Project
5

Management Unit (PMU), coordinating activities with government partners, allocating
office space and providing technical assistance to DNE staff.
DINAMA: A representative of DINAMA participated in the SC and coordinated project
activities with the DNE and the MGAP. It provided in-kind support (e.g., offices,
technical assistance, human resources and undertaking data within the project scope.)
MGAP: A representative of DINAMA participated in the SC and coordinated project
activities together with the DNE and the DINAMA. It provided human resources, offices,
information and undertaking data within the project scope.
UNIDO: In charge of overseeing project execution. It was represented by a one of its
members at the Steering Committee.
In addition, a Project Advisory Committee composed of representatives from the
private and financial sectors, associations, academic institutions and other relevant
groups was set up; it provided technical input and advice to the SC.
As mentioned above, the SC created a Project Management Unit (PMU) and selected
its members. The PMU’s tasks were: to be locally responsible for the project and the
main contact for government institutions and organizations, to develop a work plan,
and to coordinate the communication and dissemination of project results. The unit
was composed of a national coordinator, an economist, an agronomist, a chemical
engineer and an assistant, who were national advisors and were based in Montevideo.
They were hired with project resources from the GEF. The project coordinator was in
charge of the daily management and supervision of the project, including general
technical aspects, recruitment coordination and monitoring.
Apart from the SC member institutions, there were other organizations in the project.
It is important to mention those project co-financers (Estancias del Lago, ALUR,
FAROLUR and INC), who committed to large-scale investments in waste valorization
plants. On the other hand, small-scale projects were developed, for which a call for
proposals was made aimed mainly at private companies in the agricultural and
industrial sector, which received financial support to test the feasibility of different
technologies and become demonstration projects.
Other relevant stakeholders related to knowledge generation were the University of the
Republic (UdelaR), the National Dairy Institute (INALE) and the National Agricultural
Research Institute (INIA), as well as LATU, ANII, UTE, DINOT, DINAGUA,
DINAPYME, UNIT, CIU, ID, among others (see sociogram in Annex 4).
Finally, it is very important to comment on the relationship, during the last three years
of the project, between BioValor, ANDE and PAGE to carry out the Circular
Opportunities program and the Uruguay Circular Award. This program was not
included in the initial plan, it resulted from the study of support mechanisms that
detected the lack of such instruments and from the fact that the joint support planned
with CONAPROLE did not materialize, and funds were redirected. As mentioned
above, this program made it possible to open up the range of options and work with a
broader concept of circular economy, which was not planned at the start of the project
either.
6

UNIDO positioning
UNIDO has a long history of assisting countries in the implementation of industry
support programs through technical assistance and capacity building. More
specifically, the Climate Change and Energy division seeks to integrate GHG emission
reduction targets into industrial policies, improve energy efficiency, and promote
sustainable energy solutions to make industries more productive, competitive, and
resilient.
The division has experience in various renewable energy projects (including
hydroelectric, solar, wind, biomass and small-scale biogas), a long history of
cooperation with key stakeholders (both from the public and private sector, and NGOs),
and high standards of fiduciary responsibility.
In relation to Biovalor, UNIDO has been working, since 2008, on the “Observatory for
Renewable Energy in Latin America and the Caribbean”, a regional program to share
good practices and increase the use of renewable energy technologies in and outside
Latin America and the Caribbean. Apart from Uruguay, UNIDO is working in Chile and
Pakistan on projects that focus on biogas application and agro-industrial waste
valorization, which favors the exchange of experiences and learning. Along the same
lines, the first biogas laboratory in Brazil was launched in 2012, experience from which
synergies can also be generated.
Because of all these elements, the agency is well positioned to adequately support the
project under evaluation.
III. Project evaluation
A - Project Design
The design of the project is evaluated as Moderately Satisfactory. The project
preparation process included a qualified team of international consultants and the
active participation and contribution of local environmental and production leaders and
institutions. Based on these elements, the project design included an innovative
proposal in terms of institutional arrangements and budget allocation by GEF
consistent with the expected results. Similarly, key operational aspects such as project
management and monitoring arrangements were properly designed.
Also, the project was properly designed to promote a change to sustainable
development, which was Biolvalor’s ultimate goal. More specifically, and according to
the document approved by the GEF, the project's objective was:
“… to transform agricultural and agro-industrial waste into different forms of energy
and/or by-products to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, while at the same time
contributing to the development of a sustainable, low-carbon production model
supported by technology transfer and development” (UNIDO, 2013).
7

Achieving this objective required a thorough and detailed analysis of the existing
conceptual framework, identifying the main challenges, barriers, sectoral constraints
and capacities to propose activities to overcome such restrictions. These analyses
were reflected in what is known as the Theory of Change (ToC). The ToC identifies
causal relationships between outputs, results, and impacts, as well as driving factors
and barriers. Figure 1 shows a diagram of the project’s ToC.
The diagram identifies the problems detected at the start of the project and the barriers
to their resolution. As discussed above, intensification of the agricultural sector along
with the implementation of inadequate management practices exacerbated several
environmental problems, among them, inadequate waste management, nutrient and
agrochemical runoff, water pollution, and soil degradation.
There were several obstacles to the adoption of cleaner production methods without
affecting productivity; among them, there was a limited access to financing and
promotion mechanisms, lack of knowledge of possible environmental impact of
production systems and the benefits of changing them, lack of knowledge of clean
technologies, lack of access to such solutions, lack of training, inadequate incentives
[such as the promotion of polluting activities rather than environmentally friendly ones
(e.g., VAT exemption on chemical and not on organic fertilizers, oil price that may favor
generation of energy from fossil sources instead of renewables)].
Despite the above, some large producers, foreseeing stricter regulations or markets,
or as a business strategy, had already proactively implemented cleaner systems. Other
large companies met the requirements only because they were mandatory. However,
given the limited investment capacity of small and medium-sized farms, coercive
regulations were not recommended. There was also a need to address problems in a
more participatory manner, promoting exchanges among stakeholders (public, private
and the academia) to better understand the situation and to jointly identify the best
solutions. This is the area where the project becomes part of all initiatives, institutions
and measures promoting a green economy in Uruguay.
To overcome existing barriers, the project proposed several activities organized
around five components. More specifically, to address constraints in the political and
regulatory framework, the project suggested revising existing regulations and
proposing modifications to encourage waste transformation and adjustments to
existing financial support mechanisms (Component 1). Component 2, aimed at
expanding the knowledge base on waste transformation, proposed several studies,
such as pre-feasibility, and waste quantification and characterization. Component 3
suggested developing pilot projects to verify the technical and economic feasibility of
different technologies. Also, and to promote capacity building in the country, the project
suggested disseminating results from previous components through circular tours,
talks, websites, social networks, training courses and the construction of user-friendly
tools (Component 4). Finally, the fifth component was linked to monitoring global
objectives and individual components.
The complementarity and synergy between the components made it possible to
achieve the expected results. Examples are provided in Annex 5. We also believe that
there was an appropriate sequencing of activities, outputs, results and impacts. In other
words, outputs effectively generated results that contributed to the achievement of the
8

objectives set. Also, the potential risks did not materialize, thanks to the political
support and the positive response of the private sector. The driving factors were very
well exploited and were key to project development. Another relevant design aspect is
that, although an appropriate strategy was proposed for the scaling of pilots, the
planned schedule was not realistic; most of the results were obtained in the last years
of the project affecting the possibility of contributing effectively to technology
development and transfer.
9

Despite all the above, after analyzing the project’s performance and the different
components of its design, it becomes clear that the set of favorable elements pointed
out were not sufficient to compensate for various methodological and conceptual
limitations that, in turn, had a significant impact on the implementation phase of the
project. These limitations include the complexity of the objective set (see page 23), the
lack of a waste transformation goal, the lack of feasibility in terms of execution
deadlines, and the particular interpretation and implementation of the co-financing
concept required by the GEF as the financing entity.
As stated above, the objective was complex and had several subsections, two
undesirable features that made it difficult to identify the true objective of the project,
the contribution of the results and thus, the evaluation of the project's compliance with
the objective set. Regardless of the impact it had on the implementation of the project,
discussed in the Effectiveness section of this report, the objective's complexity and its
several subsections were considered an important limiting factor in the quality of the
design.
With regard to co-financing, in order to meet the requirements by GEF-5, the design
included a series of monetary and in-kind contributions from various public and private
organizations, including the MIEM, MGAP, DINAMA, INC and UNIDO at the
institutional level, and ALUR and Estancias del Lago at corporate level. As a whole,
the contribution committed by the co-financers represented the significant amount of
US$ 32.4 million, making it possible to reach the 10:1 ratio required by GEF to access
the incremental financing of US$ 3.4 million. These contributions show the successful
preparation efforts and the high degree of national commitment to the project.
However, apart from the amounts actually provided, which are analyzed in detail in the
Efficiency section, from the point of view of the project design, the constraint identified
is that a significant proportion of co-financing (94%) and the resulting emission
reduction of the project came from relevant initiatives of the business sector (ALUR
and Estancias del Lago) and the Colonization Institute, the implementation of which
was not properly linked to the project, as the design did not include the GEF financial
resources needed to ensure their implementation. Moreover, the project document
shows that a significant amount of preparation resources was devoted to the design,
feasibility and evaluation of these initiatives primarily to analyze technological options
and calculate the level of emission reduction to include it as one of the main indicators
in the result matrix (Output 3.1 of project component 3). Unfortunately, this contribution
was not sufficient to ensure the execution of investments.
B - Project Performance
Project performance is assessed on the basis of five specific parameters defined in
UNIDO's evaluation methodology: relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency and
sustainability.
B.1 Relevance
The relevance of the project is evaluated as Highly Satisfactory and considered, along
with the degree of appropriation of the activities promoted during execution, one of the
most salient aspects of Biovalor. The relevance of the project lies on the fact that it
11

contributed to partially reversing some of the environmental consequences of the
intensification of the agricultural sector along with inadequate management practices,
such as inadequate waste management, nutrient and agrochemical runoff, water
pollution, and soil degradation.
The relevance score includes several criteria and public policies, among them (i) the
importance given by the country to reducing the environmental impacts caused by
primary productive activity and associated agro-industrial processes; (ii) Uruguay’s
recognized efforts to diversify its energy mix and reduce its dependence on non-
renewable resources; and (iii) the exemplary performance of national authorities in
relation to international agreements requiring the identification, quantification and
dissemination of data related to greenhouse gas emissions. Moreover, all these
aspects are closely linked to the regulations described in Chapter II in the regulatory
and institutional framework section, which reinforces the relevance of these issues at
national level.
Uruguay’s interest in accessing GEF resources to implement the Biovalor project was
based on a number of environmental problems that required innovative and effective
mitigation actions. Eutrophication of water courses caused by erosion after decades of
agricultural production, and by nutrients from dairy farms and other sources is a very
important problem in the Santa Lucia River basin, which supplies drinking water to
approximately 60% of the national population.
Due to the low levels of waste use and recycling, waste management was already a
relevant topic, as an option to avoid sending waste to landfills or dumping it in
watercourses or land. In addition, the primary sector has been identified as the main
GHG emitter, mainly because of the enteric fermentation of bovine livestock, one of
the main productive activities that generates a significant proportion of the national
economic activity, employment, and exports.
There was already a national predisposition to advance the challenge of introducing
these changes as a way of improving the quality of life of the population and
consolidating the “Uruguay Natural” brand internationally, positioning production
systems, and thus the main export products, as environmentally sustainable.
For these reasons, the project became an appropriate mechanism to promote greater
awareness about environmental issues and to improve and implement the incipient,
but advanced, legislation in force and to strengthen the institutionality related to the
agri-environmental topics. These priority issues clearly show the significant relevance
that the actions proposed by the project had to national authorities during design and
implementation.
B.2 Coherence
Based on the arguments set out above, the proposed project activities not only reflect
a high degree of relevance, but also demonstrate a satisfactory degree of coherence
with existing national capacities, the degree of maturity of the institutions, and the legal
framework in force. At the same time, they are consistent with the need to identify and
promote concrete and sustainable instruments, incentives and technologies that
12

improve knowledge and promote the adoption of innovative practices by the private
sector without relying exclusively on regulations.
The Biovalor project was effectively and consistently coordinated with other initiatives
during its development. There were synergies with the PAGE program, through joint
funding to the Circular Opportunities program and the technical support provided. The
Sustainable Management of Natural Resources and Climate Change Project (DACC),
financed by the World Bank, was also highly aligned with Biovalor, as it provided funds
to improve effluent management on existing dairy farms in the Santa Lucia River basin
to reduce water pollution. With regard to other institutions, such as UNEP and GIZ,
although they have promoted project-related issues (by supporting, for example, the
National Adaptation Plan for the Agricultural Sector, environmental impact and quality
assessments, and the Bioeconomy Strategy) their relationship did not reach high
operational levels.
As in terms of policies, they have generally been consistent with the project, although
there were some exceptions. For example, at the beginning of Biovalor, synthetic
fertilizers were exempt from VAT, while organic fertilizers were not. In 2019, however,
organic fertilizers were allowed registration at the MGAP so that they could also also
be exempt, this process gave a significant boost to manufacturing. Biovalor also
strongly encouraged VAT exemption for compost (through technical arguments),
although the proposal was not considered acceptable by the Ministry of Economy and
Finance (MEF) arguing that compost contents are high in organic matter and not in
nutrients, and therefore would not be attractive to the agricultural sector. This could be
interpreted as a lack of coherence between environmental and economic policies,
although it would be necessary to establish whether it was a situational decision that
could be reconsidered.
Another aspect that can be considered inconsistent was the change in the regulations
by the National Administration of Power Plants and Electrical Transmissions (UTE)
with regard to renewable energies. More specifically, the pilot program Rincón de
Albano was allowed, at the beginning of the project, to sell all its energy surplus to
UTE, stated in the renewable energy micro generation decree 173/010. However, this
benefit was recently modified, by setting a cap on the amount of energy that could be
sold to UTE (companies can sell as much as they consume). This was a consequence
of Uruguay’s overproduction of electricity from its renewable energy mix.
B.3 Effectiveness
The complex objective set and approved for the project, analyzed in the design section,
appears to have been defined on the basis of the integration of the expected results
for each of the individual components proposed. Contrary to what was expected in a
project that pursues a clear and concise objective to be achieved from the
implementation of its components, in the case of Biovalor, each component
represented an objective in itself. Although this situation requires evaluating
compliance with the indicators in the results matrix, the positive consequence of this
design constraint was that it gave the project implementation the necessary flexibility
so that it could be adjusted to the real needs and opportunities of the sector. As shown
in the following paragraphs, this flexibility was understood by the participating entities
and project managers, allowing the addition of important revisions that significantly
13

contributed to the results achieved. However, this only partially compensated the
significant amount of time spent during the first two years of execution by the PMU to
materialize the co-financing projects.
To be consistent with project evaluation methodologies, this report analyzes project
effectiveness based on outputs and results considering the different components of
the original objective. Complementing this analysis, annex 7 presents the degree of
compliance of the indicators included in the original results matrix.
Waste transformation into different forms of energy and/or byproducts
Responding to different needs and possibilities, the goal of transforming waste from
agricultural and agro-industry production chains into different forms of energy and/or
byproducts became the main focus of project implementation. As part of the flexibility
described above, project implementation evolved towards the expansion of the
concept to also cover waste transformation as a form of recycling and nutrient use.
As mentioned above, even though the pilot projects supported as part of Outcome 3.2
of Component 3 were Biovalor’s most visible and recognized activity, it becomes
evident that all the project’s components contributed with specific activities that
promoted technologies for the transformation of agricultural and agro-industrial waste.
The call for projects largely exceeded the intended goal of supporting the design and
implementation of at least five small- and medium-scale projects; 19 projects were
presented, 11 of which were selected. After the corresponding pre-feasibility studies,
9 projects got into the implementation phase with technical and financial support from
Biovalor. At the date of this evaluation, the team verified that seven waste use
initiatives are in full operation, including biogas generation on dairy farms (1),
substitution of traditional fuels in industrial processes (1), production of organic
fertilizers (3), tannery (1), and production of compost and biogas from food waste in
rural areas (1). The pilot project of the biodigester in Rincón Blanco, still under
construction, should be added to the previous list; it is scheduled to be completed on
30 June 2021, using all the equipment financed by the project.

In all cases, interviews by the evaluation team to each of the beneficiaries and face-
to-face visits to a representative sample of projects showed the innovative nature of
the initiatives, the satisfactory performance, by the end of the project, of the equipment
and technologies adopted, and the unanimous expression of satisfaction with the
results obtained thanks to Biovalor’s technical and financial contributions. Apart from
the momentum generated by the monetary support provided, which complemented the
significant allocation of their own resources, the beneficiaries emphasized the technical
and “moral” support received, since project experts always believed in the
development of these technologies (even when many thought it impossible) and
supported them technically when problems arose. This is even more relevant in the
case of Ontilcor slaughterhouse, where Biovalor’s monetary contribution was very
small compared to the company’s investment (US$ 74,967 and US$ 864,000
respectively).
These projects gather, in a coordinated and efficient way, a relevant set of activities
directly related to the project. This included the preparation of feasibility studies for
each project, the identification and acquisition of the necessary equipment and inputs
(mostly imported), the monitoring by the project’s PMU experts, and technology
dissemination through the projects.
Equally important were the pilot projects; although there were considerable delays in
the planning and implementation process, they were instrumental to identify regulatory,
technical and financial barriers that could limit the feasibility and/or sustainability of
waste transformation initiatives in Uruguay, or subsequent studies and institutional
processes by Biovalor to remove or reduce the barriers identified. An example
illustrating this point would be the creation of a biogas quality standard based on
Rincón de Albano project; the standard details the requirements for biodigesters, thus
facilitating the use of this technology by other producers. Other examples of regulatory
barriers identified through composting projects were, as previously commented, lack
of VAT exemption for compost and the impossibility of exporting because of its
microbiological load. It should also be noted that, just as there are regulations that limit
the development of proven technologies, there are also others that promote it. For
example, the decree on renewable energy micro-generation, in force when Biovalor
was implemented and the regulation on waste, which establishes a disposal cost which
15

constitutes a fundamental income for the viability of composting companies1.
Additional information regarding the link between project investments and the
strengthening of the regulatory framework is included in the section “Development of
a sustainable low-carbon production model”.
In quantitative terms, even though it is impossible to analyze the degree of
achievement of this objective since there was no specific target in the results matrix,
the values calculated by the PMU suggest that the investment by Biovalor contributed
to the transformation (and subsequent valorization) of a significant volume of waste. In
fact, co-financing projects together with the pilot projects would transform
approximately 600,000 tons of wet waste per year, while approximately 3.5 million tons
of dry waste is generated in Uruguay annually. Although these figures are not
comparable, since they are expressed in different units, they allow us to estimate there
is a significant amount of waste managed by the project’s investments.

The Circular Opportunities initiative, designed and financed by Biovalor in partnership

their cross-sectional nature, the characteristics and relevance of this initiative to the

it is important to note that a significant proportion of the proposals supported aimed at
waste transformation and/or valorization. While the volume of waste with the potential
to be processed was not quantified by the proposals presented, the review of the
supported initiatives suggests that Circular Opportunities represents an innovative
mechanism with an enormous potential to promote the processing of a higher waste
volume in the country.
As mentioned above, even though the pilot projects were Biovalor’s most visible and
recognized activities, it is important to note that all components of the project
contributed with specific activities that promoted technologies for the transformation of
agricultural and agro-industrial waste. Apart from the Circular Opportunities, Biovalor
contributed to the strengthening of the regulatory framework and knowledge base, as
well as to the building of capacities to complement direct support for pilot investments
in specific technologies.
According to the analysis conducted some aspects, could have been better
implemented in the project. The first one is the relative disconnect between the types
of pilot projects supported and the information provided by Biovalor on the main
primary waste-generating subsectors. The second is the apparent lack of cooperation
and commitment by the dairy industry to the promotion of waste processing
technologies, both those generated by industrial processes and by dairy farms. Finally,
regarding the pilot projects, because of the time spent on selection and installation,
they started functioning fully in the last two years of the project, significantly reducing
the time available for implementation, measurement and evaluation of results, as well
as for the use of pilots for demonstration and dissemination of new technologies. Post-
Biovalor monitoring by the corresponding institutions seems to be the key to overcome
this obstacle.
In summary, despite the fact that waste processed by the project was only partially
measured, and even assuming that the transferred volumes are not quantitatively
significant, the actions promoted by Biovalor in connection with this objective have
18

been very effective, valuable and instrumental in developing knowledge, reducing
barriers, improving regulations, and building a new public and private awareness about
the importance and potential of waste transformation as an essential contribution to
the development of sustainable agri-food systems.
Reduction of Green House Gases Emissions
This objective is directly linked to the fact that the GEF-5 specific program that provided
funding (Climate Change Mitigation - CCM) was focused on climate change. For this
reason, it becomes one of the main indicators in the original results matrix.
The major barrier identified to fulfill this objective was the fact that the reduction of
resulting emissions was directly and almost exclusively dependent on the investments
planned with co-financing resources. Fortunately, even though the planned co-
financing investments were only partially implemented, the amount of emmissions
reduced that can be attributed to the project was similar to the values predicted in the
original matrix of results and indicators. More specifically, according to estimates by
PMU experts, the amount of emissions actually reduced was of 1,483,481 Ton CO2eq,
a 106% reduction in the target set in the project’s original document.
More specifically, four large-scale waste treatment projects were planned as part of
Outcome 3.1 of Component 3: Digestion of vinasse from bioethanol production
(ALUR), digestion of manure from the dairy sector (Estancias del Lago), co-digestion
of raw material from agro-industrial waste (Farolur), and waste treatment and project
management in small-scale livestock farms (INC). According to the information
provided, two of these initiatives were implemented (Estancias del Lago and ALUR,
partially implemented) while the other two planned investments were not made for
reasons or decisions not related to the project’s mandate and powers.

Development of a sustainable, low-carbon production model
Of the four initial objectives, developing a sustainable, of low-carbon production model
was the broadest and most ambitious. While specific investments aimed at waste
transformation and emission reduction played a significant role in achieving this
objective, the additional set of activities carried out by Biovalor complemented these
investments and made even more significant progress towards this goal. Thus, the
project’s efforts to (i) expand available information, (ii) disseminate knowledge, and (iii)
formulate appropriate policy and regulatory instruments contributed to significantly
improving the conditions for the sustainable development of low-carbon production
systems in Uruguay.
In terms of information, a diagnosis of the status and sources of agro-industrial waste
was made. The physiochemical characterization of 25 waste flows was represented in
detailed maps showing their valorization potential, with data disaggregated by
department (output 2.1 and 4.5).
Thanks to the work conducted by PMU, Biovalor also produced a series of technical
data sheets and studies analyzing technological options for waste valorization in
different sectors, some directly linked to the pilot projects (dairy, olive oil, pruning,
tanneries, and slaughterhouses), and other focused on the sectors identified as having
20

the highest waste generation (intensive pig and poultry farming, feedlot, and poultry
processing plants) (output 1.2).
In addition to the fact sheets, the PMU conducted several studies on relevant topics,
including (i) CO2 emission estimates in priority sectors (2015); (ii) Technologies for
organic fertilizer production (2016); (iii) Technologies for biogas production (2016); (iv)
Safety guidelines for biogas management (2017); (v) Alternative fuels (2017); (vi)
Efficiency analysis of different solid waste separation technologies (2018); and (vii)
Valorization of plastic waste (2019) (output 1.2).
Through the work of consultants and PMU experts, Biovalor also made a significant
contribution to the existing knowledge and promotion of low carbon systems. Research
and pre-feasibility studies (including the social, environmental and economic
dimensions) were conducted in the sectors selected to determine the most viable
waste valorization processes and business models. This work not only included the
design of pilot projects (biogas on dairy farms, composting, alternative fuels in
slaughterhouses), but also included studies related to the projects to co-
financinginanced (composting from sugar cane bagasse, and biogas generation
models in family dairy farm colonies) and other promising sectors (biogas potential on
pig farms).
In general, Biovalor’s fact sheets and technical documents were evaluated as
appropriate and timely to support the sustainable development of production models
in Uruguay including waste transformation and valorization.
According to the evaluation conducted, Biovalor’s actions to improve and
disseminate knowledge allowed for a significant number of training activities which
not only improved the performance of the public sector in relation to the topic, but also
made the private sector aware of the existing innovative technologies to reduce the
environmental impact of productive and agro-industrial processes.
Some relevant examples of the outputs generated are the development of a Waste
Valorization Calculator (http://biovalor.gub.uy/calculadora/) and two sets of tools, one
to identify opportunities for waste valorization projects and technological options, and
the other one for financing opportunities for waste valorization and the application of
successful business models (output 4.2). The calculator is a simple and practical tool
to support private sector decision-making and is available to the public on the project
website. The software developed by the PMU allows modeling various types of waste
valorization, including electricity generation from biogas; thermal energy generation
from biogas; composting; and production of alternative fuels from agro-industrial
waste. The calculator identifies GHG emissions, economic savings from replacing
traditional fuels, and estimates power generation revenues. Estimates are made from
12 productive sectors and a series of possible waste flows and technological options.
No data were presented regarding the use of this tool, however, since it was launched
during the last years of the project, it is realistic to assume that its use will increase
after project closure.
Also, an ambitious training plan was implemented, this included: dissemination
courses on the tools developed, a total of 40 workshops attended by more than 2,300
people, the implementation of a dissemination campaign and workshops to promote
21

activities on low-emission waste treatment, waste conversion into energy and waste
valorization (otuput 4.4). The participation of international experts in these training
sessions is an element to be highlighted. With regard to the quality of these training
sessions, there was no monitoring of application of the knowledge acquired by the
assistants.
Complementing the training courses implemented, a web-based knowledge
management platform was develope (www.biovalor.gub.uy), which is actively working
(outcome 4.1). In addition to specific information about the project, the platform is user-
friendly, has a valuable compendium of technical and informative content, and was
regularly updated until the closure of the project. In terms of results, there is a very
positive trend, about 200 monthly queries in 2017 and 2018, and since 2019 there was
a significant increase, reaching a monthly average of 2,400 visits. It should be noted
that the original target in the design document was 50 monthly queries.
Other pages generated and fed by the project are www.oportunidadescirculares.org,
www.foroeconomiacircular.com and www.uruguaycircular.org
In addition to Biovalor’s contribution to the generation of information and the
dissemination of knowledge, the evaluation conducted suggests that the main results
of the project regarding the promotion of low-carbon production models have been the
result of its actions of institutional coordination and its important contribution to the
development of policy and regulatory instruments, that contributed to improving
the process of policy development, adoption and regulation through better institutional
coordination, awareness raising, and knowledge generation.
In terms of joint actions and institutional coordination, two concrete initiatives actively
promoted by the project stand out:
o The creation of the Circular Economy Network, established by Biovalor in
collaboration with Transforma Uruguay, consisting of a significant number of
public, private and academic sector leading organizations, including 15 state
institutions and agencies, 7 universities, 6 civil society organizations, and 3
farmer organizations (output 4.3).
o The alignment of the project with the Plan of Action for the Environmental
Protection of the Santa Lucia River Basin, launched in 2015 aiming to reduce
pollution of the main source of drinking water for Montevideo, the largest urban
center in the country. Along this line, new actions were established to reduce
effluents discharged to the water courses by dairy farms in the basin. This
support was aimed at developing best practices and technological
developments, and promoting nutrient circularity. The project significantly
contributed to this objective, not only through the pilot projects, but also through
the financial support given to the research project on nutrient circularity on dairy
farms (described in the next section), and in particular by coordinating with the
MGAP and DINAMA in the implementation of the package of measures by
MGAP with World Bank funding; this resulted in technical and financial support
to more than 400 dairy producers in the Santa Lucia Basin to improve effluent
management. This initiative is also considered relevant from the point of view
of counterpart contributions not provided for in the project design, which partly
22

contributed to compensating unimplemented investments (the effectiveness
section of this report contains more information on this).
In terms of the development of policy and regulatory instruments, the project made
significant contributions, which allowed to strengthen the set of public actions to
promote an adequate regulatory framework for sustainable low-carbon production. In
this regard, the promotion and adoption of the circular economy are considered to be
the most significant contributions made by Biovalor.
Within the context of component 1 (Strengthening of policies and regulatory
framework), the design of Biovalor included the development of an Action Plan to
remove financial barriers to low-emission waste valorization (output 1.3). The Plan
(Transforma Uruguay, 2019) was specifically focused on the size of the circular
economy and was led by Transforma Uruguay (which no longer exists). Actions
prioritized by the Plan included the industry transition to the circular economy; the
design of a technology center in circular bioeconomics; the promotion of nutrient
circularity on dairy farms; the strengthening of circular economy capacities; and waste
volirazation.
In response to the guidelines of the Plan, Biovalor implemented two specific actions
considered of great significance for the results of the project: the organization of the
First Circular Economy Forum in Uruguay, and the subsequent elaboration and
implementation of the Circular Opportunities program.
The First Circular Economy Forum was held in Montevideo in September 2017 and
featured 25 speakers from 14 countries and more than 350 attendees. It is interesting
to note that, as part of the organization of the forum, the project conducted a national
survey that identified more than 700 local circular economy leaders in the public,
private and academic sectors.
The opinions gathered during the interviews unanimously agree that the Forum
represented a fundamental milestone for the project and for the process of introducing
the concept of circular economy in Uruguay, not only because of the level of awareness
generated but also because it was instrumental in creating strategic alliances aimed at
promoting knowledge and technological innovation, in addition to identifying specific
opportunities that would enable the business sector to consider circular alternatives in
its operations.
The Circular Opportunities program was created in 2018 thanks to a cooperation
agreement, generated by the Forum, between Biovalor and the National Development
Agency (ANDE). Applying competitive and demand-based procedures, the program
was designed to provide technical and financial support to projects incorporating
circular economy technologies. Based on periodic dissemination/motivational calls and
events, those ideas assessed as eligible receive support on three specific phases of
the productive innovation process for the incorporation of circular technology: (i)
validation of ideas; (ii) implementation and prototypes; and (iii) implementation of
projects.
The first call for proposals was made in 2018 with a budget of US$ 575,000; a
contribution of US$ 275,000 by Biovalor and US$ 300,000 by ANDE. In 2019, the
23

second call was exclusively financed by ANDE, which allocated a budget of US$ 1.0
million. Between 2018 and 2019, through a joint project evaluation and monitoring
committee, composed of Biovalor/UNIDO, ANDE and the ministries represented in
Biovalor, 48 ideas and projects were selected for technical and financial support for
US$ 1.5 million, with an estimated counterpart contribution from the beneficiaries of
US$ 2.0 million.
The program remained in force during 2020, also with funding from ANDE and support
from other institutions and initiatives, such as PAGE, and Biovalor involvement in the
selection and monitoring of projects. The budgetary constraints imposed by the
pandemic forced ANDE to significantly reduce the amount allocated to the program,
so it was only possible to support ideas validation proposals.
The following are examples of projects supported by the program:
i. Organic brick production
ii. Poultry slaughterhouses waste processing
iii. Production and marketing of Tannat grape marc flour
iv. Valorization of sheep leather
v. Thermoacoustic eco-insulation from recycled cellulose.
vi. Manufacture of furniture from recycled wind turbine blades
vii. Bio-manufacturing of materials from fungi
viii. Bioconversion of malt bagasse for poultry feed
ix. Lithium battery management and recycling
x. Recycling of silo bags for the manufacture of waste bags
While this line of work had not been explicitly planned during the project design phase,
Biovalor’s support to the promotion of circular initiatives was of great importance. It
was one of the main contributions of the project to addressing the country's
environmental priorities.
In terms of continuity, interviews with ANDE authorities highlighted Biovalor’s
contribution to the program’s creation and the growing demand from the private sector
for this type of support, justifying the institution’s decision to continue the program with
its own resources, after Biovalor’s completion.
In addition to the creation and institutionalization of the Circular Opportunities program,
the implementation of the project made it possible to make significant progress in
relation to the regulatory framework to enable the adoption by the productive sector of
technologies related to the project’s objectives.
In particular, the project technical team worked actively to improve the technical and
economic conditions for the development of a compost market. These valuable
contributions represented a complement to the three pilot projects implemented that
included waste composting (Tresor, BioTerra and Dorados del Sol), and consisted of
technical work and the support by the MGAP before the General Directorate for
Agricultural Services (DGSA) to develop the regulatory framework required to generate
competitive conditions for the production and marketing of compost as an organic input
for agricultural use, allowing the use of organic waste with the consequent reduction
of GHG emissions. More specifically, the work led by Biovalor’s PMU with contributions
24

from public and academic institutions, allowed the definition, characterization,
registration, and control for the commercialization of organic products for agricultural
use.
Biovalor’s work also included the difficult task of improving public policies to financially
encourage the adoption of technologies for waste transformation and valorization.
Considering that, for the most part, these technologies require higher investments than
conventional processes, the development of public instruments that would allow the
investor to absorb their incremental costs was a priority for the sustainable adoption
and/or replication of these technologies.
In this regard, it is important to highlight the project’s contribution to the dissemination
of the opportunities provided by the Investment Law (COMAP) regarding exemption
from IRAE taxation and to the enactment of regulations that allow industrial or
agricultural companies to reduce VAT in the purchase of machinery that mitigates or
eliminates negative environmental impacts, both of great importance for investments
in waste transformation and/or valorization.
In other cases, Biovalor's considerable efforts on this issue were not sufficient to
generate financial, credit or tax incentives. Some examples are the proposals for (i)
interest rate reduction on bank loans for “green” investments, (ii) differentiated prices
for energy produced from biogas; (iii) VAT exemption for agricultural compost; and (iv)
adaptation of tax benefits for large companies to the special tax regime governing small
and medium-sized enterprises (IMESI).
In short, while it is necessary to continue to develop appropriate instruments of
financial incentives, the actions implemented by Biovalor have allowed to combine
greater knowledge, improvements in the regulatory framework, and the adoption of
innovative incentive and promotion instruments such as Circular Opportunities directly
linked to the results achieved in waste transformation and emission reduction. These
achievements demonstrate the interest and commitment of all the sectors involved and
represent the fundamental pillars to develop an economic and environmentally
sustainable model of low-emission production in Uruguay.
A suitable system of techology development and transfer.
Project activities directly linked to this objective effectively started during the second
half of the project. Undoubtedly, these delays had a negative effect on the possibility
of the project to contribute to the functioning of a suitable system of technology
development and transfer. In spite of the limited scope of the activities supported by
Biovalor in this regard, the efforts made to incorporate the issues of waste use and
valorization, and nutrient circularity in the academic field and the areas of research and
technological transfer have been valuable and, to the extent that they are properly
applied and continued after project completion, they have the potential to contribute to
the development, adoption and consolidation of sustainable productive systems.
Beyond the collaboration of the academia in providing knowledge to support all
technical activities, Biovalor’s articulated work with the academic sector also enabled
specific outputs that accelerated the incorporation of the topics of circular economy
and waste valorization in training and research programs, created information and
25

knowledge networks for public and private decision-making, and strengthened the
technical structures responsible for developing specific support instruments for the
private sector, particularly at MGAP, DINAMA and MIEM.
In terms of academic activity, the training courses and the thesis program that received
technical and financial contributions from the project are highlighted (output 4.5). More
specifically, Biovalor supported the organization of four training courses in biogas
(School of Engineering), waste use in soils (School of Agronomy), composting (School
of Sciences), and recycling in the context of circular economy (UNIT and CEMPRE).
Regarding the support to undergraduate and post graduate theses, Biovalor called for
support on subjects of circularity in conjunction with the UdelaR and other education
centers such as ORT, UTU, UTEC and the Pando Technology Hub (PTP). As a result
of the call, 17 proposals were selected which, as illustrated in the following chart,
promoted works related to chemical, industrial and agronomic aspects associated with
the circular economy.

B.4 Efficiency
The efficiency of the Biovalor Project was assessed taking into account the use of
resources, the cost-effectiveness of financed activities, and the contribution of co-
financing to the achievement of the objectives set.
The total approved budget for project implementation was of USD 35.8 million, of which
US$ 3.39 million were to be financed by a grant from the Global Environment Facility
(GEF), and US$ 32.41 million corresponded to counterpart contributions committed
during the design phase. The analysis of the use of financial resources is based on
two specific dimensions: on the one hand, the comparison between the original
budgetary allocations and the amounts actually spent; on the other hand, the relevance
of the resources allocated to the different activities and project categories of
expenditure.
The first parameter considered was the use of resources available through the GEF
grant. In this regard, the project’s performance was very satisfactory; a total of US$
3,337,985 million (more than 98% of the funds allocated) had been disbursed at project
completion on December 31, 2020.
However, it is important to note that this financial performance could only be achieved
after an implementation period that practically doubled the 48-month originally planned
period. As Figure 4 shows, while the original plan was to complete the project by the
end of 2017, the significant delays in the first two years of implementation and
subsequent adjustments made it necessary to extend the implementation until the end
of 2020. The graph also shows the clear annual budget mismatch during the first three
years (2014-2017) where the disbursement forecast (and thus the physical execution)
was significantly and consistently lower than the original financial schedule.
A number of considerations arise from the analysis of the evolution of resource use,
and the consequent implications for the effective duration of the project. On the one
28

hand, the evaluation team considers that the originally planned 48-month deadline was
unrealistic, as it did not reflect the technical, institutional and operational complexity of
the design presented to the GEF to achieve the ambitious goals proposed. At the same
time, the significant mismatch between planned and executed expenditures in the first
three years suggests the initial delays in payment were the result of shortcomings in
the implementation procedures (e.g., lack of manuals), of the limited proactivity shown
by the project monitoring structures, and of the time spent on co-financing projects. As
already mentioned in the effectiveness section, the 2017 acceleration in
implementation, combined with an adequate fiduciary flexibility and increased
monitoring by UNIDO, led to a satisfactory level of physical and financial execution at
the end of the project.

In terms of co-financing, the analysis by the evaluation team identified a number of
considerations relevant to the financial performance of the project.
First, the structure and composition of the co-financing committed by Uruguay as part
of the project formulation responded to the need to comply with the GEF guidelines in
this regard, which required a 10:1 relationship as a condition for approving the grant.
Apart from GEF´s reasonable objectives set through this requirement (beneficiary
commitment, practical application of the concept of incrementality, resource
mobilization and catalysis, etc.), the need to comply with the requirements made
authorities supplement the “operational” contributions of the institutions involved with
investment projects from the private sector or public companies).
Based on these parameters, the formal co-financing proposal approved by the GEF
included a total of US$ 32.4 million, composed of US$ 1.9 million from the government
and UNIDO to directly support project execution (personnel, physical space,
33

equipment, and logistics) and US$ 30.5 million corresponding to three major
investment projects en Estancias del Lago (private company, biodigesters), ALUR
(state company, biodigester) and the National Colonization Institute (INC, public
agency, community biodigester project in a colony of family farmers).
Regardless of the technical considerations mentioned above regarding the impact of
the proposed co-financing model on the design and effectiveness of the project, in
terms of efficiency, the investment planned was only partially made and financial
contributions were significantly lower than expected. More specifically, the investments
of Estancias del Lago obtained excellent results, only 26% of ALUR’s investments
were made, while the INC’s ambitious project was discarded during the pre-feasibility
phase due to lack of technical and economic viability.
In spite of the above-mentioned shortcoming, it is also important to note that project
implementation generated other counterpart contributions considered of great
relevance that had not been originally estimated. These contributions partially
compensated planned investments that were not made. This category highlights the
project’s synergies with the MGAP’s support to reduce effluent pollution in the Santa
Lucia River basin, the significant contribution of beneficiaries to the financing of pilot
projects, and ANDE’s financial contribution to actions supported by the Circular
Opportunities program (see details in the chart below). According to the information
available these sources contributed with more than US$ 10 million, raising the actual
project co-financing to US$ 23.9 million, representing 73% of the original committed
counterpart.
