Complementing the project efficiency analysis, the evaluation team carried out an
estimate of the actual unit cost of emissions and waste avoided by direct project
actions. With regard to emissions reduction, it was estimated that specific investments
made as part of Biovalor totaled US$ 14.1 million. Considering that these investments
are expected to generate a reduction of 1,483,481 ton CO2eq during their useful life,
the resulting unit value would be approximately US$ 9.51/ton CO2eq.
Similarly, the application of a similar methodology allows us to estimate that the unit
cost of disposal of waste was 1.76 US$/t of managed waste (wet waste).
The project design document estimated the cost of reduction to be US$ 2.43/ton
CO2eq, taking into account emissions avoided by large projects. This measurement is
considered to be methodological incorrect as it only takes into account the grant and
not the money invested by the large projects that represented the co-financing and
were responsible for reducing a significant proportion of the emissions in the
calculation. Consequently, and since values are not comparable, it is not possible to
conclude that the project profitability was lower than what was initially expected. In the
case of waste, the design phase did not include specific estimates.
Leaving aside the comparison of final values with the initial estimates, the calculated
values seem quite auspicious, particularly in the case of waste. In fact, the current cost
of waste disposal at a landfill in Uruguay is approximately US$ 30/ton for common
waste and US$ 200/ton for hazardous waste. Therefore, the alternative of industrial
treatment may be attractive if the necessary scale is reached and if other barriers are
overcome (such as the lack of markets or regulation for new products).
In the case of emissions, using as a reference the price of the CO2eq ton in the few
international carbon markets, it is noted that current costs would not justify the
investments made in terms of emissions reduction, thus, the benefits resulting from the
transformation of waste into nutrients and/or alternative energy sources become
greatly relevant.
The implementation of Biovalor demonstrated that the reduction of GHG and waste
achieved by the technologies applied generated, in turn, multiple potential
environmental benefits and increases product access to international markets. While
these benefits are more difficult to estimate, they should be taken into account when
comprehensively assessing the impact of the project. A reduction in the degradation
of aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems allows them to maintain their ecosystem services,
including the provision of drinking water, recreational activities, the quality of water
resources, net primary production, species habitats, etc. Therefore, the cost-benefit
analysis of projects such as Biovalor should also include an estimate of the value of
maintaining such environmental and commercial services (represented in savings in
water treatment, product sales, tourism activities, and agricultural production due to
soil degradation).
In summary and based on the detailed information provided, the analysis of all factors
related to the financial execution of the project suggests that the efficiency Biovalor
can be evaluated as Satisfactory. This view is based on the sound financial and
administrative performance of the project, including the use of nearly 100% of the grant
resources, the significant results achieved with a relatively low budget, the consistent
35

distribution of resources among the different components and categories of
expenditure, and the appropriate proportion of resources allocated to the different
investments financed. As with the evaluation of project effectiveness, UNIDO’s
flexibility was a key factor in achieving such level of efficiency. The need to extend the
execution deadline, the delays experienced in one of the pilot projects and the failure
to reach the expected co-financing values represented limiting factors, but in none of
the cases they had a negative impact on the overall efficiency of the project.
B.5 Sustainability of Results
Overall, the sustainability of the project is assessed as likely. This analysis is based on
two specific dimensions: The sustainability of the investments financed, and of the
regulatory or institutional instruments generated or promoted by the project.
All the investments supported by the project (through the pilot projects and the
initiatives supported by the Circular Opportunities program); the appropriate selection
and financial contribution of the beneficiaries; the satisfactory degree of
implementation, operation and maintenance of the financed facilities and equipment;
and the positive technical and financial results achieved suggest that these
investments present an acceptable degree of sustainability and a low risk of
interrupting their operation after the project completion.
Regardless of the above, an essential element to ensure the impact and sustainability
of this dimension of the project is to enable the replication of those technologies tested.
In this sense, the fact that the investments were only made during the last two years
of project execution makes it even more necessary to make adequate use of these
experiences (and those of research projects in nutrient circularity) by institutions to
disseminate results and promote adoption by other potential users. Only in this way
will it be possible to generate an adequate level of replication of Biovalor’s experiences,
in particular with regard to the generation of biogas on dairy farms, the processing of
waste in small urban areas, the commercial development of composting, and the
generation of alternative fuels from slaughter house waste.
The second dimension of the possible impact of the project concerns the sustainability
of the instruments developed or promoted by Biovalor to include (and eventually
expand) the project area of work in public sector priorities to promote the adoption of
these practices by the private sector. In this regard, it is important to know that Biovalor
was not an isolated initiative; on the contrary, it contributed significantly to a state policy
developed since 2016 with the creation of the National Environmental System that
promotes the interaction between productive sectors and environmental protection,
water management and climate change initiatives. Thanks to the regulatory and
institutional actions promoted by the project, the existing regulatory framework is
expected to continue improving and growing stronger in the short term thanks to the
inter-institutional coordination achieved and the increase of knowledge and capacities
initiated in Biovalor, including the effective transformation of waste into energy and
other products, and promoting the reduction of GHG emissions and other negative
environmental impacts. In this regard, the training provided to ministries directly linked
to the project (i.e, MIEM, MGAP and DINAMA). The information provided suggests that
the capacities built at MIEM would promote the continuity of those relevant tasks
conducted by Biovalor PMU members, including the maintenance of web pages, the
36

coordination of actions and the monitoring of those activities that continue beyond the
project completion (regulatory framework, inter-ministerial coordination, public-private
circular economy network, pilot projects, Circular Opportunities program, and the
institutionalization of the various technical instruments developed by the PMU).
It is also relevant to mention the continuity of a considerable number of initiatives for
the promotion and transfer of technologies such as the Circular Opportunities program,
the actions supported by PAGE and FUDAEE, and the creation of the Virtual Waste
Market by the Uruguayan Chamber of Industries, Initiatives that can help consolidate
and expand Biovalor actions in the private sector. Along those lines, the projects under
way with different educational and research centers and the academic theses
supported by Biovalor also have the potential to further disseminate and expand
technological options. In the medium term, the transfer and scaling of successful
technologies to a greater number of producers and processing industries and a greater
diversification of waste and technologies involved is expected.
As a project embedded in a priority state policy, the recent change in administration
should not lead to further changes or risks to the continuation of Biovalor actions. On
the contrary, despite the budgetary situation caused by the pandemic, the importance
assigned by the new administration to environmental issues – demonstrated by the
creation of the Ministry of Environment - and to the expansion and diversification of
export markets for Uruguayan agricultural products, adopts a new meaning to respond
to the increasing demands of the main markets by promoting the development of
environmentally sustainable agri-food systems. For these reasons, the ongoing efforts
between the Uruguayan Government and UNIDO to obtain additional GEF resources
for a new project to promote the transition to a circular economy are of great
importance in strengthening and further consolidating the transformation process
initiated by Biovalor. As an incentive to this process, it would have been positive if the
necessary set of actions required to ensure the sustainability of Biovalor had been
documented through the development of an exit strategy. Perhaps this limitation could
have been compensated by further projections of the future of the project in the closing
report (MIEM, 2020, draft).
Finally, Uruguay has just received an additional contribution focused on sustainability
of environmental issues, through an additional funding of US$10 million from the
Renewable Energy Investment Fund (REIF), led by UNIDO and MIEM as its main
counterpart, this will allow further progress in the country's energy transition.
B.6. Progress towards impact
The remarkable progress made by the project in regulatory, institutional and
demonstration terms, combined with a positive evalution regarding action sustainability
allows us to estimate that the implementation of Biovalor will have a significant impact
on the environmental performance of Uruguay, through the adoption of sustainable
low-carbon production and waste processing models. In this regard, it is important to
highlight that Biovalor was not an isolated initiative; on the contrary, it contributed
significantly to a state policy strengthened since 2016 with the creation of the National
Environmental System that promotes the interaction between productive sectors and
environmental protection, water management and climate change initiatives.
37

This projection on the potential impact of the project is particularly relevant due to the
significant contribution of Biovalor’s actions to the efforts being made by the public and
private sectors to reduce eutrophication of the water courses that make up the water
system of the Santa Lucia River basin, the main source of drinking water in the country.
As long as the validation, expansion and adoption of technologies for effluent
management and use implemented in Rincón de Albano, Rincón Blanco and Estancias
del Lago are duly promoted by the competent institutions, the Biovalor experience will
become a decisive factor in the effective mitigation of priority environmental issue in
Uruguay.
The potential impact resulting from the project depends to a large extent on the
sustainability of the instruments developed or promoted for the consolidation, and
eventual expansion, of Biovalor actions within public sector priorities and the
consequent adoption of these practices by the private sector. In this regard, it is
important to highlight the fact that the investments were only made during the last two
years of project execution, which makes it even more necessary to make adequate
use of the experiences (including those generated by research projects in nutrient
circularity) to ensure the sustainability and impact of the results achieved. Only through
an active public police promoting the replication of successful experiences will it be
possible to obtain Biovalor’s expected results in biogas generation on dairy farms,
waste transformation in small urban areas, commercial development of composting,
and the generation of alternative fuels from the meat-processing industry.
Within this context and thanks to the regulatory and institutional actions promoted by
the project, the existing regulatory framework is expected to continue improving and
growing stronger in the short term thanks to the inter-institutional coordination achieved
and the increase of knowledge and capacities in the public system, especially by
MIEM, MGAP and DINAMA. The evaluation carried out allows to conclude that the
various contributions of Biovalor (regulatory framework, interministerial coordination,
public-private dialog for the promotion of innovative technologies, demonstration and
experimental projects, the Circular Opportunities program, and the institutionalization
of the various technical instruments developed by the PMU) provide a solid foundation
for achieving long-term positive impacts.
Similarly, Biovalor’s results suggest there is potential to create a even greater
environmental impact through the promotion of waste processing technologies in other
important production chains, such as industrial dairy processing plants, feed and
poultry and pig farming. In order to adapt and include the technologies promoted by
the project in these sectors, the continuity of Biovalor’s instruments of public-private
coordination, regulatory development and knowledge generation will be essential.

account whether or not additional staff in facilities were required to implement the
technology.
It should be noted that this is a general approximation of the potential impact of pilot

C.1 Gender perspective
Regarding the inclusion of the gender perspective into the project design, targets were
defined in components 1 and 4. More specifically, a minimum percentage of women’s
participation (30%) was established for the working group coordinated by
DNE/DINAMA/MGAP and responsible for project management (output 1.1). Also, all
dissemination products had to promote women’s participation and dissemination
workshops had to have at least 30% of women’s attendance (output 4.4). It was also
defined that the university working group set up to generate knowledge on waste
valorization had to have a 30% participation of women (output 4.5). Regarding the
creation of a network to ensure the sustainability of the knowledge management
platform after project completion, gender participation was ensured, but without setting
a specific goal (output 4.3).
All the targets set were achieved and in most cases exceeded, with women’s
participation was higher than expected, see table in annex 6.
An additional comment on this topic is that the project did not answer questions such
as: Do the technologies developed improve the quality of life/working conditions of
women?, are there differences in the access to technology between men and women?
An explanation for this is that these issues were not included in the scope of the project.
It would be interesting, however, to include these aspects into future projects.
C.2 Environmental and socio-economic aspects
The Biovalor project had positive impacts on several environmental aspects, including
the following:
o Through the management of approximately 600,000 ton/year of waste, it
contributed to reducing of soil and water courses pollution, which, as discussed
above, is one of the main environmental problems in Uruguay. In this regard, it
is very relevant to calculate the eutrophication potential of different nutrient
management systems on dairy farms.
o Also, the use of waste contributed to increasing the useful life of landfills.
o Likewise, lower levels of extraction of virgin natural resources caused by an
increase in recycling, reuse and valorization of waste, allowed for a more
efficient use of increasingly scarce resources.
o The technologies used in the projects allowed a reduction in GHG emissions of
approximately 126,000 ton CO2eq per year. In this regard, Biovalor also
estimated the global warming potential of the different effluent management
systems, which makes it possible to analyze the trade off between
eutrophication and global warming. In turn, it enables the assessment of
potential conflicts between international (GHG-related) and national (soil and
water degradation) commitments and objectives. The results of this analysis
showed that there are alternatives with an average eutrophication and global
40

warming potential, while systems with a low eutrophication potential have a high
warming potential and vice versa.
o Waste management also reduced odors and the number of flies in rural
establishments and in general contributed to maintaining a healthier
environment for living.
o The project generated geo-referenced information on waste generation at
national level and the maps created from these data were sent to the National
Environmental Observatory (OAN, in Spanish) at the Ministry of Environment.
Furthermore, it should be noted that until the writing this report, no unwanted
environmental effects resulting from project activities were identified.
Based on information generated by the project a cost-effectiveness analysis of the
activities performed can be conducted; there is information on the costs of pilot projects
and their effectiveness in terms of avoiding GHG emissions or reducing the amount of
waste dumped on the ground, in watercourses or in landfills. Results were discussed
in Chapter III, Section B.4.
Also, a social cost-benefit analysis should also take into account aspects such as job
generation and quality, and indirect and induced impacts. In addition, the social cost
of carbon should be used as a reference and not a market price as stated above, nor
should taxes and subsidies (such as those granted by COMAP) be included, since at
society level what constitutes a source of income for some stakeholders is an expense
for others and therefore they are compensated. This type of analysis was also not
included in the scope of this work.
However, several positive socio-economic aspects were identified:
o Most of the pilot projects were carried out in the interior of the country,
contributing to decentralization and local development.
o The biodigester implemented in the municipal dining hall in Solis de Mataojo
had a high and positive impact on the local community, increased local
environmental awareness and has the potential to be replicated in a significant
number of urban centers in the country.
o The briquetting machine that did not work with olive bones in the Tupambaé
project was transferred to a prison facility, where it is being used to produce
briquettes from sawdust, generating jobs and training opportunities for inmates.
o The project directly benefited a significant number of people, including
beneficiaries of pilot projects and entrepreneurs from the Circular Opportunities
program, researchers, university students, hired consultants, etc.
Finally, it is important to note that no conflict regarding human rights was identified.
C.3 Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E)
Monitoring and Evaluation were initially implemented by UNIDO headquarters in
Vienna, and then transferred to UNIDO's representation in Montevideo. Apart from the
appropriate technical and fiduciary monitoring carried out by UNIDO representatives,
41

M&E's important role was operationalized through the preparation of annual reports
(Project Implementation Report /PIR). A total of six annual PIRs and one final PIR
covering the last half of the project were prepared using information contained in partial
reports by the PMU (output 5.2). PIRs are informative, presenting an adequate level of
physical and financial detail and with a good description of activities implemented
during the reporting period using as a reference the matrix of products and results
planned during design. However, because of their format, the PIRs do not reflect
managerial decisions or adjustments made or necessary to address difficulties or
barriers affecting project implementation. In compliance with GEF requirements,
UNIDO also developed the tracking tools with specific indicators related to the GEF
Focal Area that provided the grant resources.
A complementary instrument to the M&E process was the Mid-term Review (MTR),
which was carried out in 2016 (output 5.3). While the MTR date (24 months after project
star) was consistent with the design document's forecast, the project implementation
status in 2016 suggests that it would have been desirable to postpone the review until
a greater degree of progress was made. However, the report is satisfactory in terms of
the level of detail provided, it identifies the achievements made, but it does not
sufficiently elaborate on the analysis of the causes of the delays recorded and the
possible recommendations to mitigate their impact. According to the evaluation team,
the MTR process was a missed opportunity to record and formalize the important
changes and adjustments experienced by the project and to establish a new
implementation schedule that would reflect the extension required to compensate for
the delays.
C.4 Project management
The design of the project considered the establishment of a coordination and technical
support unit. This unit, identified as the PMU, was led by a National Coordination and
composed of a chemical engineer, an agronomist, an economist and a project
assistant in charge of communications. Consistent with the project management
format, the PMU members were contractually accountable to UNIDO, complementing
the administration, financial management and monitoring functions of UNIDO staff in
Montevideo and in Vienna headquarters. However, being physically installed in the
offices at MIEM, the PMU not only became the face of the project, but also managed
to establish an excellent coordination with all the institutions directly linked to the
implementation of the project.
The contribution of the PMU to the project was very relevant from a technical point of
view, this was thanks, to a large extent, to the leadership and capacity of its coordinator
(reflected by her recent appointment as advisor to the new Minister of Industry and
Energy) and the commendable dedication and commitment of all its members.
Through its performance, the PMU achieved a good integration with the public and
private institutions associated with the project, and generated important technical
inputs, in addition to the adequate fulfillment of the many coordination functions
required by the project, including the important role of supporting the governance
structure, the Project Management Committee (output 1.1), which held a total of 25
meetings throughout project execution. The continuity achieved is also noteworthy,
since, with the exception of the economist, all professionals initially hired to perform at
the PMU continued in their positions until project completion.
42

The technical coordination and management work carried out by the PMU was
adequately complemented by the UNIDO Montevideo team (Representative, Project
Officer, and Administrative Assistant) which performed the fiduciary and administrative
functions required by the project in a highly satisfactory manner, in particular with
regard to the complex processes of acquisition and import of the services, equipment
and materials needed for the implementation of the pilot projects. In addition, and of
great relevance to this evaluation are the computer systems used by UNIDO and the
quality of the technical and financial information generated and made available to the
evaluation team.
Another remarkable element of the coordination team were the communication and
outreach activities at both the national and international levels. At the national level, in
addition to the creation and maintenance of the user-friendly and informative project
website, the PMU developed a significant number of communication tools. Among
these are the videos produced for each of the pilot projects and the elaboration of a
didactic video on circular economy for dissemination in schools nationwide through an
agreement with the Ceibal Plan (output 4.2). At the international level, the joint work of
the Steering Committee with the UNIDO representative enabled the organization of the
First Latin American Circular Economy Forum in 2017, an event of great significance
for the project. It was subsequently replicated in Chile and Paraguay in 2019 and 2020,
respectively. Moreover, there are current plans to replicate the Circular Opportunities
program through a UNIDO project in Paraguay. Also, the project’s experience and
results were part of Uruguay’s presentation at the GEF annual meeting held in Da
Nang, Vietnam (June 2018) and at the COP25 Climate Change Conference in Madrid
(December 2019). Other examples of recognition of the project’s performance are the
award given by UN75 at the “5th Global Interviews Awards & 5G Citizens International
Congress” and the project's participation in events related to alternative energy issues
in Brazil, Chile, Ecuador and Peru.
D – Institutional Performance
D.1 UNIDO
UNIDO Uruguay: Synergy was built between the participating institutions and the
UNIDO team in Uruguay. In addition to providing continued support in fiduciary and
administrative matters, UNIDO also made valuable technical contributions and
participated actively in the Steering Committee, particularly through the transfer of
managerial responsibilities to the resident representative. This included the flexibility
to readjust the original project design, allowing it not to focus solely on biogas
production, but also on the introduction of the circular economy concept. It is also
important to note that the activities jointly carried out with UNIDO Vienna to simplify
the procurement process of equipment enabled the PMU specialists to devote more
time to technical functions.
UNIDO Vienna: The support provided to procurement activities jointly conducted with
UNIDO Uruguay was positively evaluated . The agency adapted to the needs of the
project and made several small or medium-sized purchases, rather than a small
43

number of larger ones. The monitoring carried out from Vienna during the first years of
the project was highly appreciated.
D.2 National Counterparts
MIEM, MVOTMA (current MA) and MGAP: The joint work carried out by these
institutions on the Steering Committee was very well appreciated by all the group as
there was a strong commitment and willingness to discuss the problems to find
solutions acceptable to all parts. There was only one previous experience in this type
of coordination and it was the PROBIO project, also financed by GEF. In other
projects/topics, inter-agency coordination had not been as smooth as in this case.
Standards, incentives and a cross-cutting action plan were also created.
Steering Committee (MIEM, MVOTMA, MGAP and UNIDO): This group fulfilled the
responsibilities assigned to it and was diligent in making the necessary decisions to
adapt the project to the needs of the country (e.g., supporting the Circular
Opportunities program and promoting the circular economy). The contribution of these
institutions was fundamental to achieve a satisfactory project governance, represented
by the important decisions made during the 25 meetings organized by the PMU during
the project.
Pilot project beneficiaries: The leadership and commitment shown by the private
stakeholders were key aspects of the project's operation. All the beneficiaries
highlighted the support of Biovalor’s technical team and the monitoring carried out
throughout the project. Moreover, they appreciated their sharing of the conviction that
such investments were possible.
Universities: The academia participated through the nutrient circularity project, the
waste characterization study and the support to various degree, master and doctoral
theses on circular economy. In addition, there are university courses on nutrient
circularity, bioenergy generation and composting. The participation of the academia
was well received by Biovalor, although the process of formalizing agreements was
relatively bureaucratic and slow.
Other public stakeholders: The National Development Agency (ANDE) played a
relevant role in the Circular Opportunities program, thanks to its expertise in managing
the program and making calls for financing and funds additional to those allocated by
Biovalor. The program also expanded the range of waste treated and technologies
used, expanding the concept of circular economy and increasing, through a detailed
evaluation process, the transparency in the selection of proposals. It is for this reason
that ANDE also plays an important role in the continuity and sustainability of project
results.
D.3 Donor
Donor: It was not possible to evaluate the GEF’s performance, since, after the
evaluation and approval of the project proposal, the institution did not have an active
participation in the monitoring of the implementation process, delegating this function
to UNIDO. Even though it is considered that the GEF’s evaluation of the proposal
44

should have identified the design shortcomings described in this report, there is a
positive assessment of the organization’s programmatic approach and the continuity
and coherence shown through the sequence of environmental projects financed,
including the Uruguay Wind Energy Program (EEM), the Energy Efficiency Project, the
Biomass Electricity Production Project (PROBIO), the Project towards an Efficient and
Sustainable Mobility (MOVÉS) and the Biovalor Project, all of which have made
tangible and strategic contributions to the country’s public environmental policies.

IV. Conclusions, recommendations and lessons learnt
A. Conclusions
The Biovalor project is considered highly relevant to Uruguay and fully consistent
with the country’s existing policies and regulatory framework. To a large extent thanks
to the effective institutional articulation achieved, the efficient management of the
Project Management Unit (PMU), and the exemplary support provided by UNIDO's
local representation, the project contributed to reducing the main barriers identified in
the theory of change and showed the necessary flexibility to adapt to the changing
conditions experienced during execution as a commendable feature
Despite its complexity, the execution stage succeeded in meeting the objectives set,
reducing GHG emissions and significantly improving waste management, promoting a
shift towards environmentally sustainable development and, in particular, towards a
circular, low-emission economy model.
The fulfilment of project objectives was achieved through the synergies and
coordinated implementation of its five components. Specifically, the project
strengthened environmental policies, the regulatory framework and the knowledge
base on waste conversion and valorization, fostered private sector investments in
technologies relevant to the project's objective, developed capacities (both within
academia and the public and private sectors), and devoted considerable efforts to
communicate and disseminate the knowledge gained.
Through the implementation of pilot subprojects, four specific technologies
(composting, anaerobic digestion, nutrient circularity, and biofuels) were tested on
existing commercial operations with satisfactory results. Although not considered
sophisticated, these technologies were appropriate and innovative for the development
stage of waste valorization in the country. As such, these subprojects provided
concrete examples of success, showing the technical and economic feasibility of
adopting the technologies tested.
In terms of sustainability, the evaluation conducted makes it possible to conclude that
reasonable conditions exist for the various contributions of Biovalor to be supported
after project completion, therefore generating a solid basis for achieving positive long-
term impacts. The potential impact resulting from the project depends to a large extent
on the sustainability of the instruments developed or promoted for the consolidation
and possible expansion of Biovalor actions within public sector priorities and the
subsequent adoption of these practices by the private sector. For this to materialize,
active public interventions will be needed to continue the development of policies,
instruments, and knowledge. As well as to promote in the private sector the replication
of successful experiences to obtain results in biogas generation on dairy farms, waste
processing in small urban areas, commercial development of composting, and the
generation of alternative fuels from the meat-processing industry.
Regarding the project’s potential impact, the significant progress made by the project
in terms of regulatory framework, institutional development and technological
demonstration suggest that the implementation of Biovalor should have a significant
impact on Uruguay's environmental performance, mainly through the adoption of
47

sustainable models of low-carbon production and waste processing. This conclusion
rests on the fact that the project was inserted into an existing state policy, the 2016
National Environmental System, which aimed to address eutrophication pollution in the
country’s main watercourses and to promote the coordination between productive
sectors and environmental protection, water management and climate change efforts.
To the extent that the validation, expansion and adoption of technologies for effluent
and waste management and utilization is duly supported and promoted by the
competent institutions, the Biovalor experience will become a decisive factor in the
effective mitigation of priority environmental problems for Uruguay and the fulfillment
of its global commitments.
In operational terms, regardless of the good results achieved, there were some design
flaws that influenced to a greater or lesser extent the effectiveness and efficiency of its
execution. The complexity of the objective set, the absence of a specific waste
transformation goal, a certain lack of realism regarding the deadlines for
implementation and a particular interpretation and operationalization of the concept of
co-financing are highlighted.
In terms of implementation, there are some opportunities for improvement, among
them, the low degree of execution in the first years of the project, the lack of concrete
actions in some sectors of high waste generation (feed lot and dairy industry), the
limited dissemination of calls for expression of interest to pilot projects and their
subsequent late implementation (e.g., Rincón Blanco) where possibilities of evaluating
the different technologies tested and disseminating them to other potential
stakeholders were significantly limited.
In short, the project was Satisfactory, exceeded expectations in several of the goals
set for each component and generated several positive results, with good prospects
for future support and expansion, despite limitations observed in its design and some
other operational constraints.
B. Recommendations
o The Uruguayan government should continue promoting instruments to
encourage the replication of Biovalor’s successful experiences, especially the
generation and use of biogas, nutrient circularity on dairy farms, the use of
waste in meat processing plants and the installation of micro biodigesters in
public institutions in urban centers.
o Similarly, it is necessary to define and develop the potential to generate even
greater environmental benefits through the promotion of waste processing
technologies in other important production chains, such as industrial dairy
processing plants, feed lots, and poultry and pig farming.
o While significant progress has been made through the project, it is crucial that
the MIEM, MGAP and DINAMA continue their joint efforts to eliminate the
barriers to competitiveness and expansion of some of the technologies tested,
such as the lack of incentives for manufacturers and suppliers for equipment
and inputs linked to project technologies, and the tax burden on compost
marketing.
o The environmental authority, together with the Ministry of Economy and
Finance, should seek further dissemination of COMAP as an incentive
48

instrument for private investment aimed at waste processing and
decarbonization of production activities.
o Similarly, an inter-institutional effort is required to develop a technology and tax
framework that promotes the viability and competitiveness of bio-products,
including the development of appropriate technologies and the expansion of tax
benefits for SMEs and small farmers contributing to IMESI.
o ANDE, in coordination with Biovalor’s executing bodies, should make the
necessary efforts to ensure the continuity of the Circular Opportunities program
and to obtain a budget allocation similar to that of 2019, in line with the demands
of the private sector.
o With regard to gender, these aspects should be more explicitly mentioned in
future projects indicators.
o Because it was not possible to complete its installation before the end of
Biovalor, the pilot project of Rincón Blanco biodigester should receive an active
monitoring by DINAMA to ensure its proper completion within the agreed
deadline (30 June, 2021). Similarly, appropriate monitoring of financed nutrient-
circularity initiatives by academic and research institutions is needed to ensure
result generation and dissemination.
o It would be important to incorporate the lessons learnt from the project and to
allocate resources for specific actions to consolidate Biovalor’s results in the
design of the new GEF-financed project managed by UNIDO in Uruguay.
o In terms of project design and management, UNIDO should assess the
possibility of (i) reviewing the methodology for recruiting and including co-
financers into the project’s costs and results matrix (in compliance with GEF
requirements); (ii) providing additional flexibility for procurement procedures for
private pilot/demonstration projects; (iii) expanding the scope of the mid-term
review to identify possible restructuring needs of activities, budget allocation
and indicators; and (iv) reviewing project management criteria and
responsibilities, making the necessary adjustments to achieve greater
decentralization of project management and monitoring functions.
C. Lessons Learnt
Biovalor's implementation generated a number of valuable technical and operational
lessons which can be applied to future projects both in Uruguay and in the global
portfolio of UNIDO-supported initiatives.
Regarding project design:
• The lack of a clear objective definition may result in multiple and complex
objectives which creates unnecessary difficulties during implementation and the
subsequent evaluation of results.
• The proper inclusion of private pilot and/or demonstration subprojects requires
an adequate definition of procedures, ideally in the form of a detailed Subproject
Manual produced during project preparation, and not during the initial phase of
implementation, to maximize the operation period of financed technologies.
49

• The fulfillment of the project objectives should not be conditioned to the co-
financers’ decisions and actions, particularly in those cases where the execution
of the investment committed does not include a significant contribution of project
resources.
• The requirement of the 10:1 co-financing ratio is significantly high, because of
this, it must be duly interpreted and evaluated by the preparation team, and
incorporated into the design differentiating the co-financers' commitment from
the potential contribution of the planned investments to the outcome indicators.
Regarding project implementation:
o Institutional aspects: Biovalor demonstrated that effective inter-ministerial
coordination becomes crucial not only for an adequate governance, but also for
analyzing cross-cutting issues and generating stronger solutions agreed upon
by all parties. Also, in projects that combine regulatory and technology adoption
goals by the productive sector, the joint development of operational coordination
instruments with the academia, research activities, and the private sector is
essential to ensure the viability of the actions implemented.
o Flexibility: In order to maintain actions relevant and consistent, some flexibility
in implementation becomes critical to allow for eventual adjustments to the
original design parameters. To achieve this, a technically qualified and
committed PMU is required, together with an active and representative
governance, and the active involvement of UNIDO. The unit should work closely
with the executing institution and the Steering Committee, strengthening
existing capacities in participating bodies and transmitting analytical,
operational and technical objectivity.
o Management: The technical and operational functions of the PMU should be
duly complemented by an agile and decentralized system of administrative and
financial management, such as the one provided by the UNIDO Local
Representative team which optimized the use of time so that the Biovalor PMU
could focus on the project's technical execution.
o Monitoring and follow-up: UNIDO’s contribution to the success of the project
would have been more effective, had the mid-term review been more
comprehensive and focused on adjustments to overcome difficulties and/or had
introduced new implementation activities during the remaining execution period.
Depending on the relevance and depth of the recommended adjustments and
modifications, restructuring the original project, including indicators and closing
dates, would have allowed for the formalization of agreed changes, a better
planning of actions, and a more realistic analysis of the final results.
Regarding pilot subprojects:
• The beneficiaries’ motivation and profile represent a key factor and they should
be included in the subproject selection matrix.
• Both during project design and implementation, it is essential to define whether
the subprojects to be supported pursue a pilot objective (experimentation of
new technologies) or a demonstration objective (promoting the adoption of
proven technologies).
50

• In the case of demonstration subprojects, project support should further
promote a pre-existing intention of the beneficiary, which justifies a significant
amount of co-financing. The Biovalor experience showed that, beyond the
financial support received, the “moral”, technical and logistical support
provided throughout subproject implementation was highly appreciated by all
beneficiaries.
• It is reasonable for some pilot subprojects not to produce satisfactory results
(e.g., the Tupambaé subproject, supported by Biovalor), but demonstrating
that an application or technology is not viable can also be considered a lesson
learnt and a positive outcome for the project.
• The pilot cases also showed that there are circular economy projects in
agriculture that can offer economically viable solutions, especially if current
regulations set high costs for traditional waste disposal methods.
• The effective contribution of pilot projects to the project objectives requires a
realistic execution schedule, with enough time for the implementation and
demonstration of the proven technology, and for the technical and economic
evaluation of its results. In practical terms and according to the experience
gained, subproject selection, design and installation should not extend beyond
the first 30 months of implementation.
• In the exceptional and unforeseen event that the start-up of a pilot subproject
is significantly delayed, it is necessary to establish monitoring mechanisms
and, after project completion, disseminate the lessons learnt.
The implementation of Biovalor demonstrated that the reduction of GHG and waste
achieved through the use of these technologies generates, in turn, multiple potential
benefits in terms of environmental protection and product access to international
markets. Even though these benefits are difficult to estimate, they should be
considered by national authorities when defining appropriate regulations and
instruments, and by international agencies to comprehensively assess the outcome
and impact of projects with environmentally positive interventions. In the case of
Biovalor, reducing the degradation of aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems contributes to
maintaining their ecosystem services, including the provision of drinking water, the
quality of water resources, net primary production, species habitats, etc. Therefore, the
initial and final cost-benefit analysis of this kind of projects should also include an
estimate of the value of maintaining such environmental and commercial services
(through avoided water treatment costs, avoided losses in product sales, in tourism

lessons_learned: Flexible programme design that allows resource reallocation between technology tracks — based on demonstrated uptake and market readiness — significantly outperforms rigid activity plans in complex multi-technology green economy programmes.

Establishing financial mechanisms (green credit lines, revolving funds) early in project implementation rather than at mid-term is critical, as financial instrument setup requires longer lead times than physical technology demonstrations.

Robust baseline data collection on energy consumption and emissions at the enterprise level is essential for demonstrating project impact; without reliable baselines, attribution of GHG reductions to project interventions is contested.

Strong alignment between project objectives and national policy commitments — such as Nationally Determined Contributions — creates political ownership that sustains programme momentum through administrative transitions.

Private sector co-financing commitments in green technology projects are most successfully mobilized when complemented by risk-reduction instruments; grants alone do not address the financial barriers to first-mover technology adoption.
recommendations: The Government of Uruguay should embed the green economy financial mechanisms established under this project — particularly the green credit line — within permanent public financial institution operations to ensure their continuity beyond GEF funding.

UNIDO should support Uruguay in developing a national green economy monitoring framework with standardised enterprise-level energy and emissions reporting, enabling accurate tracking of NDC contributions from the industrial sector.

Future GEF green economy programmes in upper-middle-income countries should prioritise catalytic financial instruments over grant-based technology subsidies, recognising that the primary barrier is financial risk rather than technology availability.

The Ministry of Industry of Uruguay should use evidence from this project's technology demonstrations to update sectoral energy efficiency standards and incentive frameworks, institutionalising the transition to low-emission technologies.

UNIDO and GEF should explore a programmatic approach to green economy support in Latin America that enables Uruguay and peer countries to share technology assessment data, financing instruments, and market development lessons across country boundaries.

sdg_mapping.7: The project supported deployment of renewable energy technologies — including solar, wind, and biomass — in prioritized sectors in Uruguay, contributing to SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy) by increasing the share of renewables in the national energy mix and reducing dependence on fossil fuels in industrial processes.
sdg_mapping.9: By providing technical assistance and financing mechanisms to enterprises adopting low-emission technologies and cleaner production methods, the project promoted sustainable industrialization and innovation (SDG 9) across Uruguay's priority industrial and agro-industrial sectors.
sdg_mapping.12: The project stimulated the adoption of sustainable production practices in targeted sectors — including resource efficiency improvements, waste reduction, and clean technology adoption — advancing responsible consumption and production patterns (SDG 12) at the enterprise and sectoral level in Uruguay.
sdg_mapping.13: Low-emission technology deployment and sustainable practices in industry directly reduce Uruguay's greenhouse gas emissions. The project contributes to SDG 13 (Climate Action) by demonstrating viable pathways for industrial decarbonization and providing evidence for national climate policy development.
sdg_mapping.17: The project was funded by GEF and implemented through UNIDO in partnership with the Uruguayan Ministry of Environment, Ministry of Industry, and multiple private sector actors — exemplifying the multi-stakeholder partnerships required by SDG 17 (Partnerships for the Goals) for transformational climate action.

thematic_justification: The project's primary objective was to stimulate the adoption of sustainable practices and low-emission technologies in key Uruguayan industrial and agricultural sectors, with a direct focus on reducing greenhouse gas emissions and transitioning towards a green economy. This encompasses renewable energy deployment, energy efficiency in industry, and low-carbon production methods, placing it squarely in the Climate Action thematic area.
